# Troubleshooting SPI communication issues (2025-09-22 01:35Z)

_**User (2025-09-19 03:17Z)**_

I am having continual issues trying to get the SPI communications working bidirectionally and solidly between Daisy and ESP32.  For example:

ESP32 wants to send a BROWSE_REQ to Daisy.  It sends the request via ATTN signaling, daisy receives it.  The Daisy formulates a large data packet response, but the ESP32 doesn't receive or recognize it.  It logs only command packets arriving.

Here are some tips to examine:
Likeliest root causes (ranked)

RX buffer too small on the ESP32 side (64B)
Large responses silently truncate to 64 B (or whatever your hd-slave RX buf is), the rest arrives as a second “packet” and desynchronizes the parser.

Look for something like:

#define RX_CHUNK 64 or spi_slave_hd_rx_buffer_config(size=64)

queues initialized with 64-byte buffers.

Fix: Make RX DMA buffers ≥256B (plus header/CRC) and ensure your one transaction reads the entire BROWSE_RESP in a single CS-low window.

CS toggles mid-packet (master/Daisy)
If the Daisy HAL/LL splits the 256 B send into multiple transactions, the STM32 driver may pulse NSS between DMA chunks. Your ESP32 will treat each CS-low window as a separate “packet.”

On Daisy (STM32):

If using HAL_SPI_Transmit_DMA(), ensure NSS pulse mode is off; manually control CS as a GPIO and hold it low for the entire 256 B + header + CRC.

If using hardware NSS, check NSSPMode = SPI_NSS_PULSE_DISABLE.

In PulseView, verify CS stays low across all 256 B.

Header/payload size mismatch
If payload_len in the header says 256 but you actually send 252 (or vice-versa), the receiver waits for bytes that never come (or slurps into the next frame).

Ensure the same struct packing and byte order on both sides.

Compute CRC over the same byte range both sides agree on (see #6).

Protocol enum drift
BROWSE_RESP has different numeric values between protocol.h on Daisy vs ESP32. Then a switch(type) sends it down the wrong handler (possibly the 64-byte “command” path).

Fix: Add static asserts to both sides to lock the values (see probe below).

Struct padding / alignment differences
If your header is a struct without packed, its sizeof can differ across compilers (GCC for STM32 vs Xtensa/RISC-V). A 3-byte field gets padded to 4, etc.

Fix: Use __attribute__((packed)) (GCC/Clang) or wrap with #pragma pack(push,1)/#pragma pack(pop). Then static_assert(sizeof(Header)==EXPECTED) on both sides.

CRC computed over different slices
Classic: Daisy includes header+payload; ESP32 includes only payload; or one side includes padding/unused bytes.

Fix: In protocol.cpp, centralize compute_crc(const uint8_t* buf, size_t n) and call identically on both ends. Add a unit test (see probe).

Ring-buffer/queue copies stomp memory
A 256 B packet is queued, but after enqueue the producer reuses the same static buffer and zeros it. By the time the consumer reads it, your pretty bytes turned to zeros.

Fix: Use distinct buffers per in-flight packet, or deep-copy into the queue.

Voltage/edge and samplerate illusions
Unlikely now that you’ve decoded bytes, but still: verify CS and SCK integrity at 24 MHz sample rate. If you see odd breaks that line up with ~64 B boundaries, that’s a hint the master is chunking the transfer.

Single transaction TX on Daisy:
// Pseudocode
gpio_write(CS, 0);
HAL_StatusTypeDef st = HAL_SPI_Transmit_DMA(&hspi, tx_buf, header_len + payload_len);
wait_for_dma_done();
gpio_write(CS, 1);

Ensure one transmit, CS low the whole time. If you must chunk, keep CS low and chain DMA, don’t let NSS pulse.

What your earlier capture hints at

You posted a 256-byte blob with AA 60 early in the header, then mostly zeros. That smells like:

Magic 0xAA60

A short header

Big payload (likely directory entries, padded/empty for now)

This supports hypotheses #1 (64B RX) or #2 (CS pulsing/chunking): the small command path works; the big path breaks.
